elements.sent-2411-001: Ge'ez, an ancient Ethiopic script of cultural and historical significance, has been largely neglected in handwriting recognition research, hindering the digitization of valuable manuscripts.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our study addresses this gap by developing a state-of-the-art Ge'ez handwriting recognition system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach uses a two-stage recognition process.
elements.sent-2411-004: First, a CNN is trained to recognize individual characters, which then acts as a feature extractor for an LSTM-based system for word recognition.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our dual-stage recognition approach achieves new top scores in Ge'ez handwriting recognition, outperforming eight state-of-the-art methods, which are SVTR, ASTER,and others as well as human performance, as measured in the HHD-Ethiopic dataset work.
elements.sent-2411-006: This research significantly advances the preservation and accessibility of Ge'ez cultural heritage, with implications for historical document digitization, educational tools, and cultural preservation.
elements.sent-2411-007: The code will be released upon acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Handwritten recognition faces challenges due to varied writing styles and character overlaps.
elements.sent-2411-009: Intelligent systems using classification methods are effective in categorizing handwritten characters as humans do~\cite{plamondon2000online .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Deep learning algorithms, particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), have shown exceptional performance by automatically extracting features from raw images~\cite{lecun1998gradient .}
elements.sent-2411-011: CNNs are suitable for handwritten recognition due to their ability to learn directly from input data~\cite{moges2017study .}
elements.sent-2411-012: CNNs have become dominant in handwritten character recognition, excelling at capturing spatial hierarchies and local features in images~\cite{krizhevsky2017imagenet .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, they are limited to processing spatial information without considering sequential context.
elements.sent-2411-014: This limitation highlights the potential benefits of integrating Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), especially Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks~\cite{hochreiter1997long .}
elements.sent-2411-015: LSTMs are designed to handle sequential data and capture temporal dependencies, making them suitable for tasks where character context and order are crucial.
elements.sent-2411-016: A hybrid model combining CNNs and LSTMs can leverage the strengths of both: CNNs for local feature extraction and LSTMs for sequential processing.
elements.sent-2411-017: This integrated approach can lead to more accurate and robust recognition of handwritten characters, addressing the variability and complexity in individual writing styles and sequences~\cite{wigington2017data .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{images/New_krr.PNG} \caption{Example of a Ge'ez handwritten manuscript. This historical document exemplifies the challenges in optical character recognition (OCR) for low-resource scripts, highlighting the intricate and variable nature of Ge'ez handwriting. It underscores the necessity of advanced machine learning and deep learning techniques to accurately digitize and preserve such culturally significant texts} \label{fig:example}
elements.sent-2411-018: These challenges and approaches are particularly relevant when considering specific writing systems like Ge'ez~\cite{geez , an archaic writing system widely employed in Ethiopia and Eritrea, which poses distinctive difficulties for digital preservation and recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-019: There is a pressing need to digitize thousands of hand-written documents in Ge'ez to prevent their deterioration and ensure their long-term preservation.
elements.sent-2411-020: At present, there is a notable deficiency in efficient handwriting recognition algorithms specifically designed for the Ge'ez script.
elements.sent-2411-021: This deficiency poses a risk to the potential loss of priceless cultural and historical heritage.
elements.sent-2411-022: An example of Ge'ez document is visible in Figure~\ref{fig:example .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this work, we want to help this process by creating a sophisticated handwriting recognition system specifically designed for the Ge'ez script.
elements.sent-2411-024: We propose an innovative CNN-LSTM architecture for word-level recognition where CNNs act as feature extractors feeding into LSTM-based sequence modeling.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our work leverages a comprehensive dataset of handwritten Ge'ez characters and words, enabling thorough training and evaluation of our recognition systems.
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose a novel method based on CNN and LSTM for handwritten Ge'ez scripts;.
elements.sent-2411-028: WIth a Cer of 26.95 and a NED of 26.50, our model achieves state of the art performance for Ge'ez optical character recognition;.
elements.sent-2411-029: The paper is structured as follows: Section \ref{sec:sota presents related works in the handwritten Ge'ez recognition, Section~\ref{sec:methodology presents our method, Section~\ref{sec:experiments show the efficacy of our method presenting experiments and discussing the results, in comparison with the state of the art, and finally in Section~\ref{sec:conclusions we present the conclusions of the work.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The field of Ethiopic script character recognition, particularly for historical handwritten documents, faces a significant challenge due to the scarcity of available datasets.
elements.sent-2411-031: Before our work, the only dataset available in the literature was the HHD-Ethiopic dataset introduced by \cite{belay2023hhd .}
elements.sent-2411-032: This scarcity of data has been a major limiting factor in developing and evaluating OCR systems for Ethiopic script.
elements.sent-2411-033: The HHD-Ethiopic dataset, while groundbreaking, represents just the beginning of the necessary resources for this field.
elements.sent-2411-034: Its introduction highlights the critical need for more comprehensive and diverse datasets to advance research in Ethiopic script recognition.
elements.sent-2411-035: The limited availability of annotated data underscores the importance of our contribution and its potential impact on future studies in this area.
elements.sent-2411-036: In recent years, Optical Character Recognition systems (OCRs) have been increasingly explored for various scripts and languages.
elements.sent-2411-037: The most similar language to Ge'ez is Amharic, which shares a common script and linguistic roots.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, while Amharic OCR has seen significant advancements, the unique characteristics and lower resource availability of Ge'ez present distinct challenges that require specialized approaches in OCR development.
elements.sent-2411-039: In the work of ~\cite{belay2023hhd , the authors proposed an end-to-end Amharic OCR system utilizing traditional CTC-based architectures like Plain-CTC and Attention-CTC, incorporating CNNs and Bi-LSTMs for feature extraction and sequence prediction, with attention mechanisms to enhance recognition of complex script nuances.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Their approach was evaluated on the HHD-Ethiopic dataset, featuring historical handwritten text-line images from 18th to 20th-century Amharic and Ge’ez manuscripts, addressing the lack of resources for recognizing these complex scripts.
elements.sent-2411-041: Similarly, authors of ~\cite{belay2020amharic introduced a blended Attention-CTC network architecture for Amharic text recognition, combining CNNs and Bidirectional LSTM for feature extraction and sequence learning, with attention mechanisms directly integrated within the CTC framework on the ADOCR dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, It uses an encoder, attention, and transcription module in a unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-043: Belay et al.~\cite{belay2020ocr proposed an end-to-end Amharic OCR approach using a neural network that combines a CNN-based feature extractor, Bidirectional LSTM for sequence learning, and a Connectionist Temporal Classification transcriber.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The model, trained on the ADOCR dataset with various Amharic fonts, eliminates the need for character-level segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-045: Du et al.~\cite{du2022svtr introduced SVTR, a single visual model for scene text recognition that integrates feature extraction and text recognition in a unified framework.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Their approach involves decomposing text images into small patches, termed character components, which are processed through a three-stage architecture featuring global and local mixing blocks to capture both intra-character and inter-character dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-047: Shi et al.~\cite{shi2016end proposed a Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network (CRNN) for scene text recognition, integrating feature extraction, sequence modeling, and transcription into a single end-to-end trainable framework.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This approach uses convolutional layers for extracting sequential features, bidirectional LSTM layers for sequence learning, and a transcription layer with Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) for output prediction.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{images/w1.png} \includegraphics[width=0.15\linewidth]{images/c1.png} \caption{Ge'ez word and character in handwriting style 1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{images/w2.png} \includegraphics[width=0.15\linewidth]{images/c2.png} \caption{Same Ge'ez word and character in handwriting style 2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{images/n.png} \caption{Subset of Ge'ez characters that are challenging to discriminate} \end{subfigure} \caption{Challenges in Ge'ez handwriting recognition. (a) and (b) demonstrate the variation in handwriting styles for the same word and characters. As visible, the difference in appearance is significant, highlighting the complexity of recognition across different scribes or historical periods. (c) shows a subset of the 182 Ge'ez characters, focusing on those that are particularly difficult to distinguish due to their similar forms.} \label{fig:styles}
elements.sent-2411-049: Shi et al.~\cite{shi2018aster proposed ASTER, an attentional scene text recognizer with flexible rectification, designed to handle distorted or irregular text, such as perspective or curved text.}
elements.sent-2411-050: ASTER combines a rectification network using Thin-Plate Spline (TPS) transformation to correct distortions and a recognition network that predicts character sequences using an attentional sequence-to-sequence model.
elements.sent-2411-051: It performs on irregular text recognition without needing extra annotations.
elements.sent-2411-052: Fang et al.~\cite{fang2021read introduced ABINet, a text recognition method that models linguistic behavior in an autonomous, bidirectional, and iterative manner.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The ABINet architecture decouples the vision model (VM) and language model (LM), enforcing explicit language modeling by blocking gradient flow between them.
elements.sent-2411-054: They utilized a bidirectional cloze network (BCN) to capture bidirectional context and employed an iterative correction mechanism to refine predictions progressively, enhancing recognition of low-quality images.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/output.pdf} \caption{} \label{fig:character_predictions_characters} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/outputpp.png} \caption{} \label{fig:character_predictions_text} \end{subfigure} \caption{Examples of predictions made by the model. (a) Character-level predictions. (b) Text-level predictions.} \label{fig:merged_predictions_vertical}
elements.sent-2411-055: The OCR model architecture integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to recognize Amharic text in images.
elements.sent-2411-056: It begins with a series of convolutional layers organized into residual blocks for feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-057: These blocks incorporate residual connections to facilitate better gradient flow in the deep network.
elements.sent-2411-058: Each block is followed by max pooling to reduce spatial dimensions and dropout for regularization.
elements.sent-2411-059: Batch normalization is applied after convolutional layers to stabilize training and improve convergence.
elements.sent-2411-060: The extracted features then feed into two stacked bidirectional LSTM layers, capturing sequential dependencies in both directions of the text.
elements.sent-2411-061: Dropout is also applied between LSTM layers to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-062: A final fully connected layer produces character probabilities for each time step.
elements.sent-2411-063: This hybrid architecture leverages CNNs' strength in visual feature extraction and LSTMs' proficiency in processing sequential data.
elements.sent-2411-064: The model is trained using Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss, enabling learning from unsegmented input sequences.
elements.sent-2411-065: This approach is particularly beneficial for handwritten text recognition where character boundaries are not explicitly defined.
elements.sent-2411-066: CTC aligns predicted character sequences with ground truth, handling variable-length outputs and allowing for blank tokens or repeated characters as needed.
elements.sent-2411-067: By combining these architectural elements with various regularization techniques, the model achieves a robust and generalized approach to the complex task of Amharic text recognition.
elements.sent-2411-068: The proposed model architecture integrates CNN and LSTM networks to effectively process and recognize Ge'ez text in images.
elements.sent-2411-069: At its core, the model employs a series of four residual blocks, each comprising convolutional layers with ReLU activation and batch normalization, followed by max pooling and dropout.
elements.sent-2411-070: These blocks progressively increase the number of channels from 64 to 512, extracting hierarchical features while reducing spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-071: The residual connections within these blocks facilitate better gradient flow, enabling easier training of the deep network.
elements.sent-2411-072: Following the convolutional layers, the extracted features are reshaped and fed into two stacked bidirectional LSTM layers, each with 512 units in both directions.
elements.sent-2411-073: These LSTMs capture sequential dependencies in both forward and backward directions, which is crucial for understanding the context of Ge'ez's text.
elements.sent-2411-074: A Finally, a fully connected layer maps the attended features to character probabilities for each time step, corresponding to the Ge'ez character set, plus a blank token for CTC loss.
elements.sent-2411-075: Also, the model was trained and evaluated using a Linux operating system and GPU Nvidia RTX 3090.
elements.sent-2411-076: The computational setup ensured the efficient handling of large datasets and provided the environment for deep learning model training and performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-077: We use the HHD-Ethiopic dataset for handwritten text recognition comprises 79,684 samples, including two key test sets: an In-Distribution (IID) set of 6,375 samples drawn randomly from the training distribution, and an Out-of-Distribution (OOD) set of 15,935 samples from 18th-century manuscripts, with in the testing distribution.
elements.sent-2411-078: The IID/OOD split enables an evaluation of model performance on both familiar and historically challenging samples.
elements.sent-2411-079: We evaluate our model against several existing approaches, as detailed in Section~\ref{sec:sota , with results summarized in Table~\ref{tab:sota .}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/accuracy.png} \caption{The loss and classification accuracy of the character recognition model during training. The system seems to learn the task in a few epochs, and further training did not significantly improve accuracy.} \label{fig:loss}
elements.sent-2411-080: We employ two widely-used metrics in handwritten text recognition: Character Error Rate (CER) and Normalized Edit Distance (NED).
elements.sent-2411-081: Both metrics were also solely used in prior works, such as~\cite{belay2023hhd , as standard benchmark for evaluating similar models.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Character Error Rate (CER) is a metric used to evaluate the performance of text recognition systems.
elements.sent-2411-083: It measures the edit distance between the predicted text and the ground truth text at the character level.
elements.sent-2411-084: The formal definition of CER is: \begin{equation}CER = \frac{S + D + I}{N}\end{equation} Where S is the number of substitutions, D is the number of deletions, I is the number of insertions, and N is the total number of characters in the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-085: CER is expressed as a percentage, with lower values indicating better performance.
elements.sent-2411-086: Normalized Edit Distance (NED) is another metric used in text recognition evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-087: It's based on the Levenshtein distance (edit distance) between the predicted text and the ground truth, but it's normalized to account for varying text lengths.
elements.sent-2411-088: The formal definition of NED is: \begin{equation}NED = \frac{EditDistance(\text{predicted}, \text{ground\_truth})}{max(len(\text{predicted}), len(\text{ground\_truth}))}\end{equation} Where $EditDistance()$ calculates the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) required to change one word into another.
elements.sent-2411-089: NED is expressed as a value between 0 and 1, with lower values indicating better performance.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{The comparison with the state-of-the-art approaches, trained on HHD~\cite{belay2023hhd}. In bold, the best performant method is highlighted.} \label{tab:sota} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \begin{tabular}{lc|c|c|c|c} \multirow{2}{*}{Method Name} \multirow{2}{*}{Year} \multicolumn{2}{c}{CER} \multicolumn{2}{c}{NED} \cmidrule(lr){3-4} \cmidrule(lr){5-6} IID OOD IID OOD Human-performance\cite{belay2023hhd} - 25.39 33.20 23.78 33.70 SVTR\cite{du2022svtr} 2022 19.78 30.82 17.95 28.00 ASTER\cite{shi2018aster} 2018 24.43 35.13 20.88 30.75 ABINet\cite{fang2021read} 2021 21.49 30.82 18.11 28.84 CRNN\cite{shi2016end} 2016 21.04 29.86 21.01 29.29 Plain-CTC\cite{belay2020amharic} 2020 20.88 33.56 19.09 31.90 Attn-CTC\cite{belay2021blended} 2021 19.42 33.07 21.01 32.92 HPopt-Plain-CTC\cite{belay2023hhd} 2024 19.42 32.01 17.77 29.02 HPopt-Attn-CTC\cite{belay2023hhd} 2024 {16.41} {28.65} {16.06} {27.37} Ours 2024 15.48 26.95 15.00 26.50 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-091: We can see from Table~\ref{tab:sota , that our model obtains 15.48 for CER using the IID test set, and 27.96 for the OOD test set.}
elements.sent-2411-092: This not only surpasses existing OCR models presented in the table but also outperforms human-level recognition, which was used as a baseline for comparison in recognizing historical Ethiopic script in~\cite{belay2023hhd .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Figure~\ref{fig:loss show the loss values across epochs, and shows the accuracy of classification during train time, showing that the model converges in few epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The proposed model exhibits a strong capacity for accurately and efficiently recognizing characters in the Ge'ez script.
elements.sent-2411-095: The utilization of CNNs for feature extraction, in conjunction with LSTMs, has demonstrated its effectiveness in addressing the challenges of handwritten character recognition.
elements.sent-2411-096: This paper makes a substantial contribution to the domain of document digitalization and preservation, surpassing the previous SOTA approach, specifically for manuscripts that are not adequately represented in digital format.
elements.sent-2411-097: Future research will focus on expanding the model's capability to handle more complex script scenarios and enhancing its adaptability to process novel, unfamiliar information.
elements.sent-2411-098: Furthermore, the future direction involves exploring tiny machine learning~\cite{capogrosso2024machine and split computing~\cite{cunico2022split techniques to optimize execution and improve real-world implementation of this recognition system, making it more scalable and accessible for widespread use, particularly in resource-constrained environments.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Ge'ez, an ancient Ethiopic script with significant cultural and historical importance, has been largely overlooked in the field of handwriting recognition, limiting the digitization and preservation of valuable historical documents.
elements.sent-2411-100: This study addresses this gap by developing a novel Ge'ez handwriting recognition system that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and meta-learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-101: Our approach comprises parallel recognition processes, each optimized for different aspects of Ge'ez script recognition.
elements.sent-2411-102: We train a CNN model specifically for recognizing individual Ge'ez characters, creating a robust foundation for character-level recognition.
elements.sent-2411-103: Concurrently, we implement an innovative CNN-LSTM architecture for word recognition, where the CNN acts as a feature extractor feeding into the LSTM-based sequence modeling system.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our meta-learning framework enhances the model's adaptability: the inner loop optimizes for quick adaptation to new scribes' writing styles, while the outer loop improves overall generalization across diverse handwriting characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-105: To address the scarcity of annotated Ge'ez data, we leverage Geez Script, a comprehensive dataset sourced from an online GitHub repository.
elements.sent-2411-106: This valuable resource comprises 12,000 samples of 182 distinct Ge'ez characters and 52,000 words, representing diverse handwriting styles from different scribes.
elements.sent-2411-107: By utilizing this rich dataset, we ensure our model is exposed to a wide variety of writing styles, enhancing its robustness and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-108: On Geez Script, our method achieves 97.93\% accuracy in character recognition and 83.60\% accuracy in word recognition, outperforming previous approaches and setting the new state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-109: This work represents a significant step forward in preserving and increasing access to Ge'ez cultural heritage, with potential applications in historical document digitization, educational tools, and cultural preservation efforts.
elements.sent-2411-110: The two contributions, method and dataset, will be made publicly available upon acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-111: Index Terms—Ge'ez script, handwriting recognition, convolutional neural networks, long short-term memory, meta-learning.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{w1.png} \includegraphics[width=0.15\linewidth]{c1.png} \caption{Ge'ez word and character in handwriting style 1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{w2.png} \includegraphics[width=0.15\linewidth]{c2.png} \caption{Same Ge'ez word and character in handwriting style 2} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.8\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{n.png} \caption{Subset of Ge'ez characters that are challenging to discriminate} \end{subfigure} \caption{Challenges in Ge'ez handwriting recognition. (a) and (b) demonstrate the variation in handwriting styles for the same word and characters. As visible, the difference in appearance is significant, highlighting the complexity of recognition across different scribes or historical periods. (c) shows a subset of the 182 Ge'ez characters, focusing on those that are particularly difficult to distinguish due to their similar forms.} \label{fig:geez_challenges}
elements.sent-2411-112: Ge'ez is an ancient Semitic language that originated in Ethiopia and Eritrea by the 4th century BCE \cite{geez .}
elements.sent-2411-113: While it is no longer spoken as a first language, it remains the liturgical language of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church and holds immense cultural and historical significance \cite{ullendorff1955semitic .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The Ge'ez script, also known as Ethiopic, is used to write several languages in Ethiopia and Eritrea, including Amharic and Tigrinya.
elements.sent-2411-115: Despite its importance, Ge'ez has been largely overlooked in the field of handwriting recognition.
elements.sent-2411-116: This oversight has significant implications for the preservation and accessibility of thousands of historical documents and manuscripts written in Ge'ez.
elements.sent-2411-117: The lack of efficient handwriting recognition algorithms for the Ge'ez script poses a risk of losing invaluable cultural and historical heritage.
elements.sent-2411-118: Several scholars have emphasized the urgent need for digital preservation and recognition systems for Ge'ez manuscripts to safeguard this rich cultural legacy and cope with the risk of deterioration of ancient texts and manuscripts \cite{beyene2018digital,mahmoudi .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Recognizing handwritten Ge'ez text is also important for enhancing accessibility, allowing scholars and researchers worldwide to study Ethiopian and Eritrean history and culture \cite{ketema2021digital .}
elements.sent-2411-120: Furthermore, automated recognition facilitates the development of educational tools for learning the script \cite{assabie2009offline .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Lastly, efficient recognition systems accelerate the analysis and cataloging of historical documents, supporting comprehensive research \cite{bogale2020ethiopic .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Recognizing handwritten Ge'ez presents several unique challenges: first of all, Ge'ez script consists of 182 characters, including consonant-vowel combinations, making it more complex than many other writing systems.
elements.sent-2411-123: Additionally, characters can sometimes be connected, making it challenging to segment individual characters accurately.
elements.sent-2411-124: In terms of style, handwritten Ge'ez can vary significantly between different scribes and historical periods, adding complexity to the recognition task (see Fig.~\ref{fig:geez_challenges ).}
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, there is a scarcity of digitized Ge'ez text and annotated datasets for training recognition models.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this paper, we propose two main contributions to Ge'ez script recognition.
elements.sent-2411-127: The first is a novel CNN-based system for robust character-level recognition, designed to capture the unique features of individual Ge'ez characters.
elements.sent-2411-128: The second is an innovative CNN-LSTM architecture for word-level recognition, incorporating Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML)\cite{finn2017model for rapid adaptation to new handwriting styles, where CNNs act as feature extractors feeding into LSTM-based sequence modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-129: On GeezScript, our system achieves 97.93\% accuracy on character recognition and 83.60\% accuracy on word recognition, setting new benchmarks for Ge'ez handwriting recognition.
elements.sent-2411-130: While focused on Ge'ez, our approach provides a framework that can be adapted to other under-represented scripts, contributing to broader efforts in preserving linguistic diversity through technology.
elements.sent-2411-131: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Sec.~\ref{sec:SotA provides a comprehensive review of related work in handwriting recognition, with a focus on Ethiopic scripts and meta-learning techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Sec.~\ref{sec:methodology describes our methodology, including the dataset creation, model architecture, and training process.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Sec.~\ref{sec:results presents our experimental results and analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Finally, Sec.~\ref{sec: conclusion concludes the paper and discusses directions for future work.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Handwriting recognition has evolved with significant advancements driven by deep learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-136: This section reviews state-of-the-art approaches relevant to Ge'ez script recognition, emphasizing the need for improved methods in this domain.
elements.sent-2411-137: Early approaches, including Hidden Markov Models \cite{bengio1995lerec , Support Vector Machines \cite{cortes1995support , and k-Nearest Neighbors \cite{zhang2006svm , achieved limited success due to variability in handwriting styles and contextual challenges.}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The introduction of deep learning marked a turning point, with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) excelling in digit recognition \cite{lecun1998gradient and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), particularly LSTMs, showing strong performance in sequence modeling \cite{graves2009offline .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The use of Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) \cite{graves2006connectionist further enabled end-to-end training without explicit segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Despite advancements, recognition of Ethiopic scripts, especially Ge'ez, remains underdeveloped \cite{meshesha2007optical .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Studies in Amharic, which uses a script derived from Ge'ez, have explored HMM-based recognition \cite{assabie2011offline and CNNs for character recognition \cite{belay2019amharic , but challenges persist due to the complex syllabic structures and lack of standardized datasets \cite{meshesha2007optical .}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Transfer learning has shown potential in related scripts but remains underexplored for Ge'ez \cite{taye2019transfer .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Our research addresses these gaps by integrating CNNs and LSTMs tailored for Ge'ez, enhancing adaptability to varied handwriting styles through innovative approaches, and utilizing the comprehensive Geez Script dataset.
elements.sent-2411-144: This aligns with the broader need for robust solutions that effectively handle the unique challenges of Ge'ez script, aiming to advance recognition systems and support the preservation of this culturally significant heritage.
elements.sent-2411-145: Belay et al. proposed an end-to-end Amharic OCR system utilizing traditional CTC-based architectures like Plain-CTC and Attention-CTC, incorporating CNNs and Bi-LSTMs for feature extraction and sequence prediction, with attention mechanisms to enhance recognition of complex script nuances.
elements.sent-2411-146: Their approach was evaluated on the HHD-Ethiopic dataset, a significant contribution to Ethiopic OCR research, featuring historical handwritten text-line images from 18th to 20th-century manuscripts in Amharic and Ge’ez.
elements.sent-2411-147: This dataset, which they curated, addresses the lack of resources for recognizing these scripts, known for their intricate syllabic structures and orthographic variations.
elements.sent-2411-148: Belay et al. proposed an end-to-end Amharic OCR approach using a neural network that combines a CNN-based feature extractor, Bidirectional LSTM for sequence learning, and a Connectionist Temporal Classification transcriber.
elements.sent-2411-149: The model, trained on the ADOCR dataset with various Amharic fonts, addresses the challenges of complex syllabic structures and visually similar diacritical marks.
elements.sent-2411-150: This approach eliminates the need for character-level segmentation, enhancing recognition efficiency and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-151: Du et al. introduced SVTR, a single visual model for scene text recognition that integrates feature extraction and text recognition in a unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-152: SVTR demonstrated better performance in Chinese text recognition.
elements.sent-2411-153: Shi et al. proposed a Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network (CRNN) for scene text recognition, integrating feature extraction, sequence modeling, and transcription into a single end-to-end trainable framework.
elements.sent-2411-154: CRNN handles variable-length sequences without requiring character segmentation or predefined lexicons.
elements.sent-2411-155: Shi et al. proposed ASTER, an attentional scene text recognizer with flexible rectification, designed to handle distorted or irregular text, such as perspective or curved text.
elements.sent-2411-156: This end-to-end approach allows ASTER to perform on irregular text recognition without needing extra annotations.
elements.sent-2411-157: Fang et al. introduced ABINet, an innovative scene text recognition method that models linguistic behavior in an autonomous, bidirectional, and iterative manner.
elements.sent-2411-158: A novel bidirectional cloze network (BCN) is utilized to capture bidirectional context, improving character prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-159: Furthermore, ABINet employs an iterative correction mechanism to refine predictions progressively, which enhances recognition on low-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our contributions are the Geezscript dataset (Sec.~\ref{sec:dataset ) and the meta-learning approach to be applied for Ge'ez handwriting recognition (Sec.~\ref{sec:method ).}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Our work leverages a comprehensive Ge'ez dataset from an existing repository.
elements.sent-2411-163: Historical manuscripts: Sourced from religious and public archives, representing classical Ge'ez script typically written on parchment.
elements.sent-2411-164: These manuscripts date from the 13th to the 19th century, providing a wide historical range.
elements.sent-2411-165: Contemporary Ge'ez writings: Derived from modern publications, educational materials, and personal correspondence, representing current handwriting styles.
elements.sent-2411-166: Native speaker contributions: Handwritten samples provided by 50 native speakers of varying ages and backgrounds, ensuring a diverse representation of personal handwriting idiosyncrasies.
elements.sent-2411-167: Our dataset includes samples of 182 distinct Ge'ez characters, with multiple instances of each character to capture variations in writing styles.
elements.sent-2411-168: This diverse collection spans various geographical areas, historical eras, and demographic profiles, maximizing the variety of handwriting styles and script intricacies.
elements.sent-2411-169: The dataset is divided into training (70\%), validation (15\%), and test (15\%) sets, with care taken to ensure that samples from the same writer do not appear in different sets.
elements.sent-2411-170: Our approach leverages a meta-learning framework implemented using a combination of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-171: The process starts with the construction of a feature extractor, which is a CNN model designed to extract relevant features from input images.
elements.sent-2411-172: The feature extractor is built with several convolutional layers, max-pooling operations, and dropout for regularization.
elements.sent-2411-173: This architecture is tailored to process grayscale images resized to 128x32 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-174: The weights of this feature extractor are initialized using a pre-trained model to leverage existing knowledge and expedite the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-175: We then load these pre-trained weights into our feature extractor, ensuring that the model begins with a solid foundation of learned features.
elements.sent-2411-176: To prepare the dataset for training, we perform several steps including loading images and their corresponding labels, encoding the labels, and normalizing the images.
elements.sent-2411-177: Images are resized and converted to a format suitable for the model, while labels are encoded using a label encoder to prepare them for the Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss function.
elements.sent-2411-178: The encoded labels are padded to ensure consistency in length, and the images are normalized to improve training stability.
elements.sent-2411-179: The core of our approach utilizes the MAML algorithm to enhance the model's ability to generalize across different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-180: The MAML framework is implemented with two primary components: an inner loop for task-specific updates and an outer loop for meta-optimization.
elements.sent-2411-181: In the inner loop, the model performs a small number of gradient updates on a subset of task-specific data to adapt to the unique characteristics of each task.
elements.sent-2411-182: In the outer loop, meta-optimization is performed by aggregating the losses from the inner loop across tasks, computing meta-gradients, and updating the model's initial parameters.
elements.sent-2411-183: This dual-loop structure allows the model to quickly adapt to new tasks with minimal fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-184: The meta-learning approach enables our system to efficiently learn from a diverse range of Ge'ez handwriting styles and adapt to new, unseen styles with minimal data, which is particularly beneficial for historical document recognition where labeled data can be scarce.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Capture.PNG} \caption{Diagram of the meta-learning approach, depicting the workflow from the feature extraction to the MAML-based meta-optimization process.} \label{fig }
elements.sent-2411-185: Figure \ref{fig illustrates the meta-learning approach, showcasing the integration of preprocessing, feature extraction, and the MAML framework.}
elements.sent-2411-186: These preprocessing steps and augmentations help the model improve its ability to generalize to unseen data by simulating a wide range of handwriting scenarios it might encounter in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our model architecture consists of two main components: a CNN for character recognition and an LSTM network for sequence modeling.
elements.sent-2411-188: These are combined through a meta-learning framework to enable rapid adaptation to new handwriting styles.
elements.sent-2411-189: The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is designed to extract features from grayscale images of Ge'ez characters and transform them into a feature vector for classification.
elements.sent-2411-190: The architecture of the CNN includes several key components: The network begins with an input layer that processes grayscale images of size 28x28 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-191: It is followed by a series of convolutional layers with increasing filter depths—32, 64, 128, and 256 filters—each using 3x3 kernels.
elements.sent-2411-192: These convolutional layers are interspersed with max pooling layers that utilize 2x2 kernels and a stride of 2 to down-sample the feature maps, thereby reducing spatial dimensions and introducing translation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-193: After the convolutional and pooling layers, the model flattens the feature maps into a one-dimensional vector.
elements.sent-2411-194: This vector is then passed through two fully connected layers, with 512 and 256 units respectively.
elements.sent-2411-195: Both fully connected layers use ReLU activation functions and are followed by dropout with a rate of 0.5 to reduce the risk of overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-196: The final layer is a dense layer with a softmax activation function that outputs probabilities for each of the 182 Ge'ez characters.
elements.sent-2411-197: To enhance the training process, data augmentation techniques are employed.
elements.sent-2411-198: These techniques include rotation, width and height shifts, shear, and zoom, which help to increase the variability of the training data and improve model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-199: The model is trained with a batch size of 32 over 20 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-200: Performance is evaluated on a test set to assess the accuracy and effectiveness of the model in recognizing and classifying Ge'ez characters.
elements.sent-2411-201: This CNN architecture is designed to capture hierarchical features, from simple edges in early layers to complex character shapes in deeper layers.
elements.sent-2411-202: The LSTM network in our Geez handwriting recognition system plays a crucial role in processing the sequence of feature vectors extracted by the CNN to predict whole words.
elements.sent-2411-203: This component of our architecture is designed to effectively capture the temporal dependencies inherent in handwritten text, allowing the model to consider context when making predictions.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our LSTM architecture begins with reshaping the CNN output into a sequence of 32 time steps, each corresponding to a specific region of the input image.
elements.sent-2411-205: We implement two stacked bidirectional LSTM layers, each consisting of 256 units in both forward and backward directions, resulting in a total of 512 units per layer.
elements.sent-2411-206: The bidirectional nature of these LSTM layers allows the model to process the input sequence in both forward and backward directions simultaneously, capturing context from both preceding and following characters in Geez script.
elements.sent-2411-207: To mitigate overfitting and improve the model's generalization capabilities, we incorporate dropout layers after each LSTM layer with a dropout rate of 0.25.
elements.sent-2411-208: This means that during training, 25 The final component of our LSTM architecture is a dense layer with softmax activation, producing character predictions at each time step.
elements.sent-2411-209: This layer ensures that the output at each step can be interpreted as a probability distribution over the possible characters in the Geez script.
elements.sent-2411-210: To enhance our model's adaptability to various handwriting styles, we implement the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework.
elements.sent-2411-211: In our implementation, each task within the MAML framework involves recognizing words from a distinct subset of the dataset, simulating different handwriting styles or historical periods.
elements.sent-2411-212: The MAML process is structured around two nested optimization loops: an inner loop and an outer loop.
elements.sent-2411-213: The inner loop simulates the process of rapid adaptation to a new task by performing gradient updates on a support set, which is a small subset of the task-specific data.
elements.sent-2411-214: The outer loop is responsible for optimizing the model's initial parameters by minimizing the expected loss over all tasks after their respective inner loop adaptations.
elements.sent-2411-215: We use the Adam optimizer for both inner and outer loops, with a learning rate of 0.001.
elements.sent-2411-216: This choice balances the need for quick adaptation in the inner loop with stable meta-learning in the outer loop.
elements.sent-2411-217: The use of Adam's adaptive learning rate properties is particularly beneficial in navigating the complex optimization landscape of meta-learning.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our training process leverages the MAML framework to enhance the model's ability to adapt to new handwriting styles quickly and effectively.
elements.sent-2411-219: We begin by preparing the dataset, splitting it into 100 tasks for meta-learning, with each task containing 50 samples.
elements.sent-2411-220: These samples are further divided into support and query sets, allowing the model to simulate the process of learning from a small support set and evaluating on a query set within each task.
elements.sent-2411-221: The model undergoes meta-training for 100 epochs, with tasks processed in batches of 5 for computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-222: During each epoch, the model iterates through the tasks, performing inner loop adaptations on the support sets and outer loop updates based on the performance on query sets.
elements.sent-2411-223: We employ Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss for training.
elements.sent-2411-224: CTC loss is particularly well-suited for sequence prediction tasks like handwriting recognition, where the alignment between the input (image) and output (text) is not known beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-225: It allows the model to learn the alignment between input sequences and label sequences automatically, without requiring explicit segmentation of the input.
elements.sent-2411-226: Our evaluation strategy for the Geez handwriting recognition model focuses on two main aspects: a quantitative accuracy metric and qualitative visual inspection of the results.
elements.sent-2411-227: The primary quantitative metric we employ is word-level accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-228: After training, we use the model to make predictions on a set of sample images and compare these predictions to the true labels to calculate the proportion of correctly recognized complete words.
elements.sent-2411-229: This word-level accuracy provides a straightforward and interpretable measure of the model's performance for practical applications of Geez handwriting recognition.
elements.sent-2411-230: Complementing this quantitative metric, we implement a visualization function for qualitative assessment.
elements.sent-2411-231: This function generates side-by-side comparisons of the original images, true labels, and model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-232: This visual inspection capability is invaluable for identifying patterns in the model's performance, understanding where it excels and where it struggles, and gaining insights into how the model handles different handwriting styles or particularly challenging characters.
elements.sent-2411-233: Our experimental setup for the Geez handwriting recognition system uses TensorFlow and Keras for model implementation.
elements.sent-2411-234: The input images are standardized to a size of 32x128 pixels with a single channel (grayscale).
elements.sent-2411-235: We configure the MAML framework with 100 tasks, each containing 50 samples, providing a diverse set of learning scenarios for the meta-learning process.
elements.sent-2411-236: The learning rates for both the inner loop (alpha) and outer loop (beta) of MAML are set to 0.001.
elements.sent-2411-237: We train the model for 100 epochs, processing tasks in batches of 5 to maintain computational efficiency while ensuring a variety of tasks in each update step.
elements.sent-2411-238: The model architecture consists of a CNN with 3 convolutional layers (32, 64, 128 filters) using 3x3 kernels, ReLU activation, and 2x2 max pooling, followed by 2 bidirectional LSTM layers with 256 units in each direction.
elements.sent-2411-239: Dropout with a rate of 0.25 is applied after the convolutional and LSTM layers to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-240: We use Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) as our loss function, which is well-suited for sequence prediction tasks like handwriting recognition.
elements.sent-2411-241: This comprehensive experimental setup, combined with our detailed evaluation strategy, allows us to thoroughly assess and refine our Geez handwriting recognition model, aiming to produce a system capable of accurate and robust recognition of diverse Geez handwriting styles..
elements.sent-2411-242: The character recognition model demonstrated high accuracy in identifying individual Ge'ez characters.
elements.sent-2411-243: Fig. \ref{fig1 shows examples of character predictions made by the model.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{download3.png}} \caption{Examples of character predictions made by the model.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-244: The confusion matrix (Fig. \ref{fig2 ) provides a detailed view of the model's performance across all character classes, helping to identify which characters are most frequently confused with others.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{download.png}} \caption{Confusion matrix for the character recognition model.} \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-245: Analysis of the confusion matrix reveals that certain characters with similar visual structures are more prone to misclassification.
elements.sent-2411-246: For example, characters with subtle differences in diacritics or stroke patterns often cause confusion, particularly when the handwriting is less clear or the characters are connected.
elements.sent-2411-247: The word recognition component of the system also performed well, though with slightly lower accuracy than character recognition due to the complexity of sequential dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-248: The model achieved an accuracy of 83.60\% on word recognition tasks.
elements.sent-2411-249: The model evaluation findings demonstrate a commendable proficiency in accurately identifying Ge'ez script characters.
elements.sent-2411-250: The model's strong accuracy and F1-scores in most classes indicate its resilience and ability to handle the intricacies of recognizing handwritten script.
elements.sent-2411-251: However, the analysis also highlights certain challenges, such as specific characters that are more susceptible to misclassification.
elements.sent-2411-252: The utilization of CNNs for feature extraction, in conjunction with LSTMs for sequence processing and MAML for rapid adaptation, has demonstrated its effectiveness in addressing the challenges of handwritten character recognition.
elements.sent-2411-253: This paper makes a substantial contribution to the domain of document digitalization and preservation, specifically for manuscripts that are not adequately represented in digital format.
elements.sent-2411-254: Future research will focus on expanding the model's capability to handle more complex script scenarios and enhancing its adaptability to process novel, unfamiliar information.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
